chapters.title: A single IQ number misses what the mind can really do
chapters.text:
In modern Western culture, many people treat intelligence as a single trait that can be measured with one number, the Intelligence Quotient, or IQ. But the brain runs many kinds of intelligence in relative isolation, which makes a single score a poor summary of what the mind can do.

Anything worth teaching can be shown in many ways by drawing on multiple intelligences. Dr. Gardner groups the many forms of human intelligence into seven kinds. They are linguistic, musical, logical-mathematical, spatial, bodily-kinesthetic, intrapersonal, and interpersonal. His theory says that once we accept the existence of many intelligences, it is easier to understand why some people have certain talents and others do not. Knowing that musical intelligence exists, for example, makes it easy to see why singers like Beethoven or Andrea Bocelli have such a sharp command of voice and sound.

Brain scientists and geneticists are mapping the wide variety of human abilities. Computer programmers are building systems that are intelligent in their own ways. Educators are starting to accept that their students have different strengths and weaknesses, as Dr. Howard Gardner has noted. More research into multiple intelligences could make it possible to design new tests that spot a child's strengths and limits, which would help teachers draw out the best in every student.

This summary is a good fit for scientists, philosophers, and anyone curious about human social behavior and emotion.

chapters.title: The current view of intelligence holds the mind back
chapters.text:
The modern view of intelligence has spread so far that it sounds like a tidy list of measurable traits. It works well as a way to describe how a person thrives in a given field, and it is especially useful in the areas Dr. Gardner has studied. He laid out a list of brain abilities to back up the idea of multiple intelligences.

With the right encouragement, enrichment, and teaching, most people can grow their intellect to a solid level. Gardner admits that his list does not cover every possible angle, since he sees intelligence as a whole with many levels of analysis. A complete list would only be possible if he focused on a single field of study, like neurophysiology.

Why try to define intelligence at all when it is relative and shaped by so many factors? For example, the measured intelligence of someone in a poor country is likely to score lower than that of someone in a wealthy country. Education, poverty, and other factors all leave a deep mark on test results. Intelligence varies from person to person, but it is still possible to draw broad patterns from many examples. The chapters that follow explain the kinds of intelligence we have and how they can help us understand our own minds.

chapters.title: Many intelligences can help people reach their full potential
chapters.text:
Researchers do not agree on whether intelligence is inherited. Some experts say genetics can account for up to 80 percent of cognitive scores, while others put the share closer to 20 percent. A few even argue that IQ cannot be drawn as a straight line at all.

Given that, should we drop the language that treats talent as a fixed inheritance? We can say some people are born with the right wiring for a given skill, but that does not mean the skill will appear. Someone could be born with the ability to play chess well, but without a chessboard, that ability will go nowhere. Put that person in a place where chess is everywhere and the talent can bloom fast.

From a biological view, the environment can help a person reach a high level of skill in certain areas. The science of inheritance is complex, and it is hard to separate what came from genes from what came from the world around us.

Knowing when and where to learn is key to mastering a new skill. Canalization and plasticity are two ideas from neurobiology that help us understand how the brain works. Canalization is when a biological system grows in a predictable way. The human nervous system, for example, starts to mature in the neural tube of an embryo and then moves into place to form the brain and spine.

Many environmental factors shape development, which is called plasticity. Even if a child's brain is hurt in the first year of life, they can still learn to communicate later. Language is one of the most flexible parts of the brain. As we age, however, brain plasticity drops, which is why an adult who loses the left hemisphere can struggle with language.

The theory of multiple intelligences matters because it can help society get the most out of every child's brain. Teachers, for example, can use early knowledge of a student's interests to design a better learning path.

chapters.title: Linguistic intelligence means a deep feel for how language works
chapters.text:
Language comprehension is one of the most studied kinds of intelligence from a neurobiological point of view. Researchers know a good deal about how language skills develop, from a baby's first words to the long sentences a child can use by age four or five.

People speak many languages across every culture, so those with high language intelligence can express themselves in words and in body more easily. The language used by songwriters, composers, and poets matters a great deal to them. They pay close attention to the many shades of meaning in a word, which makes it easier to write with simple words in fresh ways. They also think about how a word can echo through other lines and which words sound good together.

Poetry is just one use of language ability. Rhetoric, for example, leans on language to persuade people to act, which is what politicians do to sway public opinion. Linguistic intelligence can also help people understand ideas and metaphors, among other things.

Most people's language skills live in the left hemisphere of the brain. Damage to specific parts of that hemisphere can knock out certain language skills. People with damage to Broca's area in the frontal lobe, for example, often write in a style that echoes Ernest Hemingway's iceberg theory, with a simple surface hiding a deeper meaning. Linguistic intelligence is a key part of communication. Conversations and speeches become clearer when people have a strong grip on their language.

chapters.title: Musical intelligence is about sensitivity to the qualities of sound
chapters.text:
Most research links musical intelligence to a person's ability to communicate through speech and sound. We can sharpen our musical intelligence by learning about harmonic tones in different ways.

Musically gifted people hear and spot patterns very quickly. Their brains think in terms of style. They pick out patterns in new music to help them learn. They also look for patterns in the way people speak, like lyrics and rhymes. Musically intelligent people have a strong sense of what music is really about.

Melodic composition is the peak of musical intelligence in the same way that poetry is the peak of linguistic intelligence. Even though only a small number of people will ever work as composers, many people can grasp the basic structure of music. They can then match pieces with similar patterns and rhythms.

Music, like language, depends heavily on the auditory system. The way the brain handles and stores pitch is different from the way it handles other sounds, like speech. In her research, psychologist Diana Deutsch asked volunteers to remember a series of tones. She then added other sounds to the original tones to keep them engaged. When the added sounds were made of many tones, the error rate was about 40 percent. When she replaced the tones with words or numbers, the error rate dropped to just 2 percent. The takeaway is that skill with sound and rhythm alone is not enough to build musical intelligence. Mastery of words and numbers also plays a big part.

chapters.title: Logical-mathematical intelligence is about conceptual thinking
chapters.text:
Logical-mathematical intelligence is the ability to analyze problems in a logical way, to do math, and to detect scientific patterns. It also includes the ability to build equations and proofs, run calculations, and work through abstract ideas.

Swiss psychologist Jean Piaget laid out an early version of this idea. As a child, he was given a circle of objects in front of him. He counted them and got 10. He then pointed to the objects in a different order and counted 10 again. After many such tests, he saw that the number 10 stood for the whole set, no matter what order the objects were in.

Mathematical intelligence helps with abstract, symbolic thinking, step-by-step reasoning, and both inductive and deductive thinking, and it boosts overall brain performance. Because of his early tests, Piaget understood numbers from a young age, and the process leaned on his logical-mathematical skills. The first step was interacting with the physical world, which grows more abstract with time and eventually crosses into logical and scientific territory.

When young mathematicians apply their knowledge to real problems, they often face a long chain of math statements. Remembering the links in the chain can help, but mathematicians are not known for their memory. Their grasp of the logical links between statements and the overall meaning is what helps them solve hard questions and equations.

The power of mathematical abstraction is hard to overstate. The field works with irrational numbers, paradoxes, and possible and impossible worlds, which forces the mind to draw parallels between analogies. The brain basis of math skills is still debated, but it seems centered in the left hemisphere. Dementia, for example, hits the left hemisphere, which can cause logical-mathematical skills to break down. Children with Gerstmann syndrome often have trouble recognizing their fingers and telling left from right.

chapters.title: Spatial intelligence helps you see and move through the world
chapters.text:
Picture a large animal like a horse and try to figure out which point is higher, the tip of its tail or the base of its head. As a second test, fold a piece of paper in half three times and try to work out how many rectangular shapes the folds create.

When you try these tasks, you will likely know right away whether they come easily. Although the two tests look very different, they both draw on spatial intelligence. They call on mental imagery and a sense of space. Spatial intelligence is the ability to read images accurately, to adjust that image in your mind, and to recreate it even when there is nothing in front of you.

Spatial intelligence shows up in people who can understand and adjust patterns in open spaces and in tight ones. These people can also recognize and handle both large and fine spatial images. Mental imaging, abstract awareness, graphic and creative skills, and active imagination all support spatial intelligence.

Most people think about sight, but spatial intelligence can work even if you cannot see at all. Blind people can have excellent observation skills, even with the loss of sight. People born blind cannot see some features of the world, like color, but they can still judge the size and scale of objects through other senses, like hearing.

Even without sight, spatial ability helps people find their way around and back to where they started. It also sharpens awareness of the small details in art, like paintings or sculptures. It does not matter what the spatial ability is used for. This kind of intelligence shows up in every culture, though some people lean on it more than others. Merging into traffic and picturing the shape of the solar system are two everyday uses of spatial intelligence.

A point worth knowing. Since the brain's plasticity drops with age, picking up new languages gets harder the older you get.

chapters.title: Bodily-kinesthetic intelligence shows up in skilled body use
chapters.text:
Bodily-kinesthetic intelligence is the use of the whole body, or parts of the body, to solve problems or build things. The process can include using the hands or the mouth.

People with bodily-kinesthetic intelligence use their bodies and minds together to pull off skills or solve problems. In many cultures, the way the body moves has little to do with intelligence, since we tend to define intelligence as the ability to think in a logical way. A closer look shows that many thinking tasks have a physical side. A surgeon, for example, has to move with great care to do the work.

You can train the body to take calculated risks through bodily-kinesthetic intelligence, even when you are not thinking about every move. Famous hockey player Wayne Gretzky would hold the puck for an extra second in front of the net, which threw the goalie off rhythm. That move gave Gretzky time to read the goalie before taking the shot. When he was criticized, he said no one would accuse a doctor of learning the job by instinct, so they should not expect that of him either.

It is also wrong to say that the brain has no role in physical movement. The brain is involved, but as a tool for the body rather than the goal itself. The central nervous system helps refine, redirect, and adapt motor activity to match a person's goals. In short, the body and the brain keep talking to each other to carry out a motor task.

Damage to specific parts of the brain, especially in the left hemisphere, can limit the ability to move and perform motor tasks. The different types of apraxia are an example. A person with apraxia understands what is being asked of them and is physically able and willing to do it, but they cannot actually do it.

chapters.title: Other-people smart and self-smart shape us as people and as a team
chapters.text:
Reading other people well means picking up on what drives them, what they want, and what they prefer. Strong readers of others tend to collaborate smoothly. Turning the lens inward, self-smart is about building a working map of who you are and using that map to steer your own choices.

It is worth being clear about the difference. There are several paths to self-awareness. You have to be able to sit with your own thoughts, feelings, and moods. The French writer Marcel Proust, a novelist, critic, and essayist, is a classic case. His sweeping work In Search of Lost Time is a deep dive into the inner world.

Reading other people, on the other hand, is about picking up on their emotional states, personality, possible motives, and intentions. Mahatma Gandhi is a useful example. He had a gift for grasping where people were coming from and guiding them toward a different view.

Smooth communication depends on knowing how to sort your own thoughts and how to speak to others in a way that fits the situation. Both forms of people-smart live in the frontal lobes, where input from the senses mixes with input from the limbic system. Put simply, the frontal lobes are where your reading of other people meets your own emotional data.

Everyone shares the same brain base for these intelligences, but the way they play out in different cultures varies a great deal. The same is true for the other intelligences, which show up in different shapes in different places. Religious traditions, for example, shape the worldview of their followers, so two people from different faiths can look at the same world in very different ways.

Self-smart and other-people smart quietly shape how we see the world. Society often mirrors who we are, so it pays to know how to read yourself well and how to read the people around you with care.

chapters.title: Final thoughts on multiple intelligences
chapters.text:
Studying the many kinds of intelligence goes a long way toward showing how the brain works and how we can tune our neural system to handle things that once looked impossible. The study of the brain is one of the cornerstones of science, and humans have been able to correct some cognitive errors through scientific and psychological research.

Dr. Gardner's view of intelligence shows that we can have diverse skills in isolation or together. Even though the brain does the heavy lifting of processing information, a fault in one area does not have to drag down the rest. He built the theory of multiple intelligences to show that people are not born with all of their intellect. The theory pushed back on the older idea that general intelligence is only about cognitive ability.

People both benefit from and fall victim to changes in their time. A person with one set of skills can be a brilliant mathematician or scientist in one era. Those skills are exactly what that era needs, but they may matter far less in the next, as Dr. Howard Gardner has noted.

Gardner's model may have grown in popularity because of a simple and attractive idea. Everyone has their own pattern of strengths and weaknesses, and research backs that up. Many people find it more useful to focus on the variety of skills than to assume people only differ in a single number on a test.

A small exercise to try. Set aside time to learn a new skill. Put real effort into getting better at it. Practice often and stay open to feedback.
